content:
7 min read 11 hours ago -- What really happens when bootstrapApplication() runs? Most Angular developers know that an application starts inside main.ts. A typical file looks like this: import { bootstrapApplication } from '@angular/platform-browser'; import { AppComponent } from './app/app.component'; import { appConfig } from './app/app.config';bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) .catch(error => console.error(error));It looks like one small function call. But that call creates the foundation on which the entire Angular application runs. It configures the application-level dependency-injection environment, registers global providers, creates the root component, connects it to the page, and starts rendering the component tree. Understanding this process helps answer several common Angular questions: • Why is a service shared across the entire application? • Why did one component receive a different service instance? • Why is a provider available inside one feature but not another? • Where are the router and HTTP client configured? • Why does everything begin with AppComponent? The answers start at bootstrap. main.ts is the entry point There is no main() function that you write to start an Angular application. Instead, the generated JavaScript executes main.ts, which eventually calls: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig);The first argument identifies the root component: AppComponentThe second argument contains application-level configuration: appConfigTogether, they answer two important questions: Where should the component tree begin? Which services and framework features should be available to the application? The smallest possible Angular bootstrap A minimal standalone Angular application can start like this: import { bootstrapApplication } from '@angular/platform-browser'; import { AppComponent } from './app/app.component';bootstrapApplication(AppComponent) .catch(error => console.error(error));Suppose AppComponent is defined like this: @Component({ selector: 'app-root', standalone: true, template: `

Hello Angular

` }) export class AppComponent {}And index.html contains: Angular uses the selector from AppComponent to locate the matching host element. It then creates the component and renders its view inside . The element is only the host. Angular creates everything that appears inside it. What happens during bootstrap? At a high level, the process looks like this: main.ts executes ↓ bootstrapApplication() is called ↓ Application-level providers are configured ↓ AppComponent is created ↓ Its view is attached to ↓ Initial rendering begins ↓ Child components and routed views are createdLet us break that down. 1. Angular creates the application environment Most applications need more than a root component. They also need features such as: • Routing • HTTP communication • Authentication • Error handling • State management • Interceptors • Animations • Third-party services These are commonly configured through application-level providers: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, { providers: [ provideRouter(routes), provideHttpClient() ] });Angular creates an application-level dependency-injection environment containing these providers. Services registered here are available across the application unless a lower-level injector provides another value for the same dependency token. This is why bootstrap configuration is an architectural decision. It determines what is globally available. 2. Angular creates the root component After configuring the application environment, Angular creates AppComponent. It is important to understand that Angular is not compiling your original TypeScript file inside the browser at this stage. The TypeScript and component templates were already processed during the build. A simplified view is: Build time: TypeScript + Angular templates ↓ Compiled JavaScript and rendering instructionsRuntime: Compiled JavaScript ↓ Component instance and rendered viewWhen bootstrapApplication() runs, Angular uses the generated code to create the component instance. 3. Angular attaches the component to the page Angular reads the root component’s selector: selector: 'app-root'It then finds the matching element: The component’s view is attached to that host element. That is the moment the Angular application starts becoming visible to the user. 4. Angular begins rendering the component tree Suppose the root template contains: Angular creates the components referenced by that template. The resulting component tree might look like this: AppComponent ├── HeaderComponent ├── RoutedPageComponent │ ├── SidebarComponent │ └── ContentComponent └── FooterComponentAppComponent is at the top because it was passed to bootstrapApplication(). Every component rendered beneath it becomes part of that application’s component tree. Angular creates more than one structure An Angular application does not only have a component tree. Several structures exist at the same time. The component tree The component tree represents how the interface is composed: AppComponent ├── HeaderComponent ├── DashboardComponent └── FooterComponentThis tree follows the relationships created by templates and routed views. The dependency-injection hierarchies Angular also maintains dependency-injection hierarchies. Two important levels are: • Application and route-level environment injectors • Component and element-level injectors This is why provider placement changes the behaviour of a service. The same service class may produce different instances depending on where it is registered. Root-level providers Consider this service: @Injectable({ providedIn: 'root' }) export class AuthService {}providedIn: 'root' makes the service available from the application’s root dependency-injection environment. Components and services throughout the application can normally share the same instance. This is useful for services such as: • Authentication • Global state • Logging • Application configuration • Shared API communication Component-level providers Now consider this component: @Component({ selector: 'app-editor', providers: [DraftService], templateUrl: './editor.component.html' }) export class EditorComponent {}Here, DraftService is provided at the component level. The resulting instance is available to EditorComponent and its descendants. It is not automatically shared with unrelated components elsewhere in the application. If Angular creates multiple EditorComponent instances, each one can receive its own DraftService. That can be useful when the service represents local component state. It can also cause bugs when the developer expected the service to be global. Route-level providers Routes can also define providers: export const routes: Routes = [ { path: 'admin', providers: [AdminStateService], loadComponent: () => import('./admin/admin-page.component') .then(module => module.AdminPageComponent) } ];AdminStateService is scoped to the admin route and its child routes. It does not automatically become available throughout the entire application. This gives us a useful mental model: Application-level provider → broadly available across the appRoute-level provider → available inside a route branchComponent-level provider → available inside a component subtreeThe service class may be identical. The ownership and lifetime of the instance depend on the injector in which it is provided. Why app.config.ts exists Instead of putting all configuration directly inside main.ts, modern Angular projects usually separate it into app.config.ts. import { ApplicationConfig } from '@angular/core'; import { provideHttpClient } from '@angular/common/http'; import { provideRouter } from '@angular/router';import { routes } from './app.routes';export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = { providers: [ provideRouter(routes), provideHttpClient() ] };Then main.ts remains small: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) .catch(error => console.error(error));This separation keeps each file focused. main.ts starts the application. app.config.ts contains application-level configuration. Components and services contain business logic. A realistic production configuration A production application might look like this: export const appConfig: ApplicationConfig = { providers: [ provideRouter(routes), provideHttpClient( withInterceptors([authInterceptor]) ), { provide: ErrorHandler, useClass: GlobalErrorHandler } ] };And main.ts: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) .catch(error => { console.error('Angular failed to start:', error); });These providers configure the environment in which the application runs. The router makes routed navigation available. The HTTP client can be injected into services. The interceptor participates in outgoing HTTP requests. The custom error handler can process Angular errors. All of this begins at bootstrap. Why the bootstrap promise should be handled bootstrapApplication() returns a promise. Bootstrapping can fail if, for example: • A required provider cannot be resolved • A provider throws during construction • The root host element cannot be found • An application initializer fails That is why Angular projects commonly use: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig) .catch(error => console.error(error));Without error handling, a startup failure may appear only as an unhandled promise rejection and may be harder to diagnose. The .catch() does not make bootstrap succeed. It ensures that the failure is visible. Standalone bootstrapping versus NgModules Older Angular applications often start like this: platformBrowserDynamic() .bootstrapModule(AppModule) .catch(error => console.error(error));In that model, AppModule acts as the root Angular module. Modern standalone applications usually use: bootstrapApplication(AppComponent, appConfig);Standalone bootstrapping removes the requirement to create a root NgModule just to start the application. But it does not remove Angular’s architecture. Angular still has: • Dependency injection • Provider scopes • Component trees • Route configuration • Application-level configuration Standalone APIs remove some ceremony. They do not remove the systems underneath it. Common misunderstandings “main.ts contains the application logic” It should not. main.ts starts the application. Business logic belongs inside components, services, and feature code. “Every Angular service is automatically a global singleton” Not necessarily. The provider location determines where the instance is available and whether another injector may create a different instance. “Standalone Angular removed dependency-injection hierarchies” It did not. Standalone APIs reduced the need for NgModules, but Angular’s hierarchical dependency-injection model still exists. “The browser compiles AppComponent during bootstrap” It does not compile your original TypeScript source. The Angular build process prepares the executable JavaScript before deployment. “Bootstrap is synchronous” It is not. bootstrapApplication() returns a promise, which is why startup errors should be handled. The key takeaway bootstrapApplication() looks like one small line, but it establishes the foundation of the entire Angular application. It identifies the root component. It configures application-level providers. It creates the application environment. It attaches the root view to the page. It begins rendering the component tree. And it creates the injector structure that determines where dependencies come from and how widely their instances are shared. Most Angular development happens after bootstrap. But understanding bootstrap explains why the rest of the application behaves the way it does. When a service has an unexpected second instance, check where it was provided. When a dependency is unavailable, inspect the injector boundary. When a feature should be global
